Q1: I kept things pretty simple and consistent. I'd block out about three hours on weekday evenings and longer sessions on weekends, totaling around 22 hours weekly. I didn't need fancy planners or apps—I just knew when I had to study and did it. It wasn't exciting, but it worked. I'd go through readings, take notes by hand, and then work through assignments methodically.
Q2: I stuck to regular times mostly. Sundays were always my big study days, and I'd do lighter review on Tuesday and Thursday evenings. Deadlines didn't really change my pattern much because I was already ahead of schedule most of the time. I find comfort in routine—it takes the guesswork out of everything, even if it feels monotonous.
Q3: Honestly, I only logged in when I absolutely had to—checking for new assignments, submitting work, or seeing if grades were posted. I didn't browse around or explore extra content. If there was a notification about a deadline, that would prompt me to log in. Otherwise, I'd forget the LMS existed for days at a time.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd download what I needed at the start of each module and then work offline. Logging in felt like a chore rather than something natural. I'd do it twice a week maybe, just enough to stay on top of requirements.
Q5: The readings and assignment guidelines were the most useful. I'm a read-write learner, so having clear written instructions and downloadable PDFs worked perfectly for me. I'd print them out or save them to my tablet and annotate them extensively. The discussion boards weren't really my thing—I posted when required but didn't find them particularly valuable.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos. Over two hours of video content sounds exhausting, and I'd rather read the same material in half the time. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions beyond what was mandatory. It's not that I thought they were useless, I just found them boring and preferred to study independently.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assessments or helped me understand core concepts. If I could see how it would improve my grade or deepen my understanding of something testable, I'd do it. Anything that felt like busywork or social padding—I'd skip it or do the bare minimum.
Q8: Yes, completely different. Required activities got done no matter what, even if I was bored out of my mind. Optional activities? I almost never touched them. I didn't have the motivation to go above and beyond when I was already putting in enough hours to get good grades. Why do extra when you're already succeeding?
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my studying happened offline. I'd download all the readings at once, print key documents, and work from my notes and textbooks. I also used external resources like academic articles from the library database. The LMS was just a delivery system for me, not where actual learning happened.
Q10: Probably most of my study time, honestly. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes, re-reading printed materials, and working through practice problems on paper. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. My best thinking happened away from screens, at my desk with a cup of tea.
Q11: That was basically my standard approach. I'd access a module's materials once, download everything, and then never look at it online again. I'd study those materials for days or weeks offline. The LMS would show one login, but I'd have spent ten hours with that content.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I was managing six online courses simultaneously, and even with good time management, the sheer volume was draining. I felt bored and unstimulated by most of the content. My stress was high, but I just pushed through mechanically. The peer support intervention helped a bit, but I was mostly running on autopilot.
Q13: Fatigue definitely affected things. When I was exhausted, I'd do the absolute minimum on the LMS—submit what was due and log off. I wouldn't explore, participate in discussions, or watch supplementary content. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also meant I had to be strategic about where I spent my energy.
Q14: Many times. I'd have readings open on my screen but my mind would be elsewhere. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd complete tasks just to check them off, not because I was genuinely engaged. It was going through the motions.
Q15: I'd sometimes delay discussion posts until the last possible moment because I found them tedious. I knew participating was important for my grade, but I had zero enthusiasm for it. I'd also postpone watching videos indefinitely, telling myself I'd get to them eventually, which I rarely did.
Q16: Deadlines were the main reason I logged in at all. I'd check when things were due, work backwards to plan my offline study time, and then log in only to submit. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have touched the LMS for weeks. They structured my entire interaction with the online system.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to keep doing what I was doing since my grades were good. But it didn't really change my engagement patterns. I'd read comments, feel briefly satisfied or note areas to improve, and then continue with my established routine. It wasn't transformative—just confirmation I was on track.
Q18: The clear structure helped me plan efficiently. I knew exactly what was expected each week, which let me batch my work and stay ahead. But the structure also felt rigid and uninspiring. I appreciated the predictability while finding it contributed to my boredom. It was functional but not engaging.
Q19: Having all materials downloadable made engagement easier for my learning style. What made it harder was the emphasis on video content and discussion participation—neither suited how I learn best. The course seemed designed for people who like interactive, social learning, which isn't me at all.
Q20: Maybe they're exploring, trying to figure out what's expected, or they're anxious and keep checking things without actually producing work. Or they could be struggling with the material and spending lots of time without making progress. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That's basically me. I performed well because I studied extensively offline using downloaded materials and my own notes. My low LMS activity doesn't reflect my actual engagement with the content—it just shows I didn't need the platform to learn. The work happened elsewhere.
Q22: Logs can accurately show submission patterns, login frequency, and time spent on the platform. But they completely miss offline study, depth of understanding, and the quality of engagement. Someone could spend hours on the LMS mindlessly clicking, while someone else downloads one PDF and masters the content.
Q23: Being engaged means actually thinking about and understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, making connections, and retaining information. For me, engagement happens during deep reading and note-taking, not through platform interactions. It's internal, not measurable by clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline study sessions, my detailed handwritten notes, or the hours I spent with printed readings. It wouldn't capture that I was bored but still disciplined, or that I found the content unstimulating despite performing well. My real academic life happened mostly outside the system.
